text: The patient is positioned for the procedure, with four robotic ports and one air cell port visible on the abdomen, facilitating access for the robotic instruments.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral border of the duodenum, a critical step that facilitates the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein, ensuring the remnant colon can reach for a tension-free colorectal anastomosis following radical rectal resection.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral border of the duodenum, a key step that facilitates the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein, crucial for achieving tension-free colorectal anastomosis following radical rectal resection.

The surgeon dissects superiorly and laterally through the bursa omentalis after dividing the inferior mesenteric vein at the inferior border of the pancreas, taking special care to separate the pancreas from the left mesocolon.

The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein at the inferior border of the pancreas, then continues dissection superiorly and laterally through the bursa omentalis, carefully separating the pancreas from the left mesocolon.
The surgeon performs a peritoneal incision to access the inferior mesenteric artery. The anterior mesenteric artery is dissected up to its origin, ligated, and then divided.
The surgeon performs a peritoneal incision to access the inferior mesenteric artery. The anterior mesenteric artery is dissected up to its origin, ligated, and then divided.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the rectum following the completion of the colonic dissection, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the rectal tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the rectum following the completion of the colonic dissection, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the rectal tissue from surrounding structures.
Following the completion of posterior dissection, the surgeon begins lateral and anterior mobilization of the rectum, using robotic instruments visible through the ports.

Following the completion of posterior dissection, the surgeon begins lateral and anterior mobilization of the rectum, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the tissue from surrounding structures.

The surgeon completes total mesorectal excision and transects the specimen at the level of the anal canal. The specimen is then removed from the surgical field. Subsequently, a double-staple colorectal anastomosis is performed, joining the remaining colon to the rectal stump.
The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision and transects the specimen at the level of the anal canal. The specimen is then removed, and a double-staple colorectal anastomosis is performed.
